coredata.dc:title: Wastewater purification with different precursors of carbon dots dominated titanium dioxide: Mechanism insight
coredata.dc:creator: Shen, Shen
Li, Huimin
Fu, Jia jia
Wang, Hong Bo
coredata.dc:description: Controlled deposition of co-catalysts on the requisite surfaces is highly crucial for myriad applications related to photocatalysis. However, the complex preparation process and different precursors may affect their photocatalytic performance. Here, carbon dots (CDs)-doped TiO2 nanosheets (CDs/TNs), which possess high photocatalytic activity and wastewater purification ability, were synthesized using different precursors of CDs (citric acid: CA and ammonium citrate: AC). Then, the prepared CA-CDs/TNs and AC-CDs/TNs were compared using different techniques. The results illustrated that the particle sizes for AC-CDs and CA-CDs are 1.50 ± 0.204 nm and 3.98 ± 0.37 nm. Compared with AC-CDs, the lattice spacing of CA-CDs is 0.195 nm, which is narrower than that of AC-CDs. Meanwhile, CDs are successfully doped on the surface of TNs. Both CA-CDs/TNs and AC-CDs/TNs exhibit outstanding photodegradation activity and robust stability. And the photocatalytic performance of CDs/TNs increases firstly and then decreases with increasing the amount of CDs. Specially, 0.2CA-CDs/TNs exhibit the highest degradation efficiency, having removed ~91.6% of CR (2 h), 90.2% of RhB (2 h) and 96.1% of TC (1 h), which are 5.70%, 7.13% and 6.7% higher than that of 0.2AC-CDs/TNs. Meanwhile, the removal performance of bright green (BG) and azorubine (ZB) were also performed. As expected, CDs activate TNs to remove BG (91.08% within 150 min) and ZB (86.69% within 4 h) more efficiently. Finally, the feasible degradation pathways of the pollutants are discussed.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Controlled deposition of co-catalysts on the requisite surfaces is highly crucial for myriad applications related to photocatalysis. However, the complex preparation process and different precursors may affect their photocatalytic performance. Here, carbon dots (CDs)-doped TiO2 nanosheets (CDs/TNs), which possess high photocatalytic activity and wastewater purification ability, were synthesized using different precursors of CDs (citric acid: CA and ammonium citrate: AC). Then, the prepared CA-CDs/TNs and AC-CDs/TNs were compared using different techniques. The results illustrated that the particle sizes for AC-CDs and CA-CDs are 1.50 ± 0.204 nm and 3.98 ± 0.37 nm. Compared with AC-CDs, the lattice spacing of CA-CDs is 0.195 nm, which is narrower than that of AC-CDs. Meanwhile, CDs are successfully doped on the surface of TNs. Both CA-CDs/TNs and AC-CDs/TNs exhibit outstanding photodegradation activity and robust stability. And the photocatalytic performance of CDs/TNs increases firstly and then decreases with increasing the amount of CDs. Specially, 0.2CA-CDs/TNs exhibit the highest degradation efficiency, having removed ~91.6% of CR (2 h), 90.2% of RhB (2 h) and 96.1% of TC (1 h), which are 5.70%, 7.13% and 6.7% higher than that of 0.2AC-CDs/TNs. Meanwhile, the removal performance of bright green (BG) and azorubine (ZB) were also performed. As expected, CDs activate TNs to remove BG (91.08% within 150 min) and ZB (86.69% within 4 h) more efficiently. Finally, the feasible degradation pathways of the pollutants are discussed.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Water pollution from organic molecules is particularly troubling [1,2]. Organic pollutants include polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons and other harmful substances [3–5]. Once entering the water, it is hard to decolorize, and long-term accumulation can cause serious harm to human beings. With the great advancement of global textile industries, anthropogenic emissions of organic dyestuffs to the aquatic environment are rapidly increasing [6–9]. Consequently, dyes have become typical industrial organic pollutants during textile dyeing and printing processes [10–12]. Along with organic dyes, tetracycline (TC), has been concerned for a long time as primary pollutants that directly affects aquatic environment [13,14]. Refractory TC in water bodies is normally generated from effluents of industries such as pesticides and pharmaceuticals [15,16]. Due to their complicated structure, it’s more difficult to damage these pollutants which are inherently chosen to resist to sunlight and to aerobic oxidation by microorganisms [17,18]. Therefore, the growing requirement for high-eﬃcient technique with purified release of organic pollutants is a major driving force pushing for the development of non-toxic, stable and high efficiency technologies [19,20].

Recently, engineering of nanomaterials along with advancements of sustainable and renewable technologies were performed [21]. Nanomaterials with different applications can be divided into nano biomedical materials [22], nano energy storage materials [23], nano magnetic materials [24], nano optoelectronic materials [25–27], and nano sensitive materials [28], ea tl. Photocatalysis, relies on sunlight energy and photocatalysts (nano optoelectronic materials) without mechanical energy consumption and no secondary pollution, has sparked explosive interests in dealing with environmental pollutants [29–31]. However, the restricted utilization of sunlight conversion suspends the industrial application of photocatalysts [32–34]. Particularly, the production of electron-hole pairs in semiconductors upon irradiation and their take part in subsequent redox reactions are the primary topics among the extensive investigated photocatalytic technique [35–37]. Moreover, e- transfer to oxygen molecule existing ubiquitously in the photocatalysis has been regarded as the rate-determining step in semiconductor photocatalysis [38–40]. Thus, the sluggish electrons transfer is the primary factor for the less utilization of photo-induced charges [41,42]. Howbeit, elements doping can modulate the capacity of photocatalysis by extending the spectrum response and promoting charges separation and transfer rate as well as generating more surface reactions [43–45]. Co-catalysts not only can capture and transmit e- eﬃciently as well as accelerate the charge separation, but also lower the activation energy or overpotential for the reactions. Thus, co-catalysts (Ag, Pt, Cu and other noble mental) are massively used to ameliorate the photocatalytic eﬃciency [46–49].

Titanium dioxide (TiO2) as a common photocatalyst utilizes sunlight to power photocatalytic process for industrial applications including organic contaminant removal in atmosphere or aquatic environment [50,51]. But the rapid recombination of photo-induced charges and the lack of absorption above 400 nm of pure TNs still compel it unsatisfactory for practical requirements [52,53]. Hence, it is inevitable to load metal or non-metal co-catalysts on pure TNs to obtain a high efficiency in spatial charge separation and transfer [54,55]. Taking energy-saving and low cost into consideration, metal co-catalysts are too sparse and expensive to be employed for large-scale production [56,57]. Therefore, pursuing a noble-metal free and highly active co-catalyst is highly desirable. Carbon dots (CDs), as attractive co-catalysts sensitive to sunlight, have been heavily investigated for wastewater purification due to their unique optical property and favorable biocompatibility [58,59]. Specifically, CDs present excellent performance of up-conversion fluorescence, photoluminescence and low photobleaching, which are prone to various photocatalysts modification to enlarge the absorption region and enhance the photocatalytic efficiency [60]. However, in most cases, the photocatalytic activity is closely related to the existing precursor selections. Therefore, modifying photocatalysts with a suitable precursor not only changes the kinetics under sunlight illumination but also offers more visible-light activity that is absent with pure photocatalysts [61].

In this work, carbon dots/TiO2 nanosheets (CDs/TNs) were prepared by doping carbon dots (AC: ammonium citrate or CA: citric acid) on TiO2 nanosheets. ( Scheme 1). We anticipate that CA or AC may show different synthesis processes and crystal structures when forming into CDs. Thus, the microstructures, crystalline phases, and optical performance of the prepared samples were compared via a series of techniques. Subsequently, CR, RhB, and TC as target pollutants were used to investigate the photocatalytic abilities of the prepared photocatalysts.

Tetrabutyl titanate (TBOT), citric acid (CA), ammonium citrate (AC), congo-red (CR), bright green (BG), azorubine (ZB) and tetracycline (TC) were provided by Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (China). Sodium hydroxide (NaOH), hydrofluoric acid (HF), and rhodamine B (RhB) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (China). Ammonium hydroxide (NH4OH) was purchased from J&K Scientific Ltd. (China). Deionized (DI) water used in the experiments was obtained using an ULUPURE pure water/water system.

TNs were prepared according to the previous report [62]. Briefly, 3 ml of HF was dispersed into 25 ml TBOT to form a uniform solution under stirring. Then the solution was transferred into a teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 24 h. After cooling down, the white powders were gathered by centrifugation, and then washed with 0.1 M NaOH and DI water for several times. Finally, the obtained powders were dried in a vacuum oven at 60 °C for 12 h.

CA-CDs/TNs and AC-CDs/TNs were procured by hydrothermal method. For CA-CDs/TNs, 1.051 g (0.2 M) of CA was dissolved into 25 ml DI water to form a homogeneous solution under constant stirring. Next, the pH value of the above solution was adjusted to 6 by NH4OH. After that, 0.4 g of TNs was dispersed and sonicated for 1 h. Then the precursor mixture was placed into a teflon-lined autoclave and kept in 160 °C for 4 h. Finally, the precipitates were collected and washed with DI water, and dried at 60 °C for 12 h. CA-CDs/TNs composites with different amount of CA -CDs (corresponding to 0.5255 g, 0.7883 g, 1.051 g, 1.3138 g, and 1.5765 g of CA), respectively abbreviated as 0.1 CA-CDs/TNs, 0.15 CA-CDs/TNs, 0.2 CA-CDs/TNs, 0.25 CA -CDs/TNs, and 0.3 CA-CDs/TNs, were prepared.

For comparation, AC-CDs/TNs composites were prepared using AC as precursor of CDs. Briefly, AC-CDs/TNs composites were synthesized using the same method except that AC rather than CA and NH4OH. Following the same method, CDs/TNs composites prepared with different amount of AC-CDs (0.608 g, 1.216 g, 1.824 g, and 2.432 g of AC) were labeled as 0.1 AC-CDs/TNs, 0.2 AC-CDs/TNs, 0.3 AC-CDs/TNs, and 0.4 AC-CDs/TNs, respectively.
AC-CDs and CA-CDs were synthesized by the same hydrothermal method as described above without adding TNs.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) carves of samples were recorded using an X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker AXS) with Cu Kα radiation. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra results were characterized by a Thermo Nicolet IS10 spectrometer scanning from 4000 to 500 cm−1. The microstructures of prepared samples were observed by Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) using a JEM-2010, JEOL instrument. UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) patterns were performed on a UV-3600 (Agilent, Cary300) instrument with BaSO4 as a reference. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data was achieved on a Thermo ESCALAB 250XI device.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were performed with a CHI-760E electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chenhua Instrument Co., Ltd, China), with a frequency range from 100 kHz to 0.1 Hz and an amplitude of the modulation signal was 5 mV. Photocurrent was measured using a conventional three-electrode system with 0.5 M of Na2SO4 as the electrolyte solution, Ag/AgCl as the reference electrode, and Pt wire as the counter electrode. The working electrode was prepared by spreading a slurry of the as-prepared photocatalyst onto the fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) glass.
The degraded products of CR, RhB, and TC were identified via LC-MS (Waters, USA) with a BEH C18 column (1.7 µm, 2.1 *150 mm). Further details are portrayed in the Supporting information.

To simplify the experiment, 0.2CDs/TNs were selected for further comparison since 0.2CDs/TNs perform the highest photodegradation property after doping CDs on TNs. During photocatalytic process, abundant electrons will be stimulated from the (valence band) VB to the (conduction band) CB of photocatalysts only if the energy of the motivated light is equivalent to or greater than the photocatalysts’ band-gap energy. Otherwise, the photocatalytic process will be restricted or not appear. Thus, the optical property of TNs, 0.2CA-CDs/TNs and 0.2AC-CDs/TNs were evaluated by DRS. As shown in Fig. 2, TNs exhibit the intrinsic absorption with the wavelength shorter than 400 nm, suggesting that pure TNs can only absorb UV light. However, CA-CDs/TNs and AC-CDs/TNs not only show stronger UV light absorption, but also have an increased absorption performance in visible light spectrum, confirming that both CA-CDs/TNs and AC-CDs/TNs can capture visible light. Notably, CA-CDs/TNs present greater absorption intensity in the range of 400–750 nm compared to AC-CDs/TNs, illustrating that CA-CDs/TNs may produce a large number of electrons and holes under visible-light illumination.

The microstructures of TNs, CDs, and CDs/TNs were discerned unambiguously by TEM. The images of TNs are shown in Fig. 3a-b. Pure TNs present rectangular sheet-shape structure. And the interplanar spacing of 0.2105 and 0.356 nm are considered as the (001) and (101) planes of TNs. Fig. 3c and e illustrate that the prepared CA-CDs and AC-CDs are spherical with a tiny agglomeration. Importantly, CA-CDs (inset in Fig. 3c) present a lattice spacing of d= 0.225 nm, which is bigger than that of AC-CDs (d=0.195 nm). From Fig. 3d-f we can see that both CA-CDs and AC-CDs are distributed on the surface of TNs uniformly, suggesting CDs and TNs are coupled together. The size of CDs has a great influence on the catalytic and optical performance of photocatalysts. Fig. 3g and h are the particle size of AC-CDs and CA-CDs. AC-CDs are uniform with the size of 1.49 ± 0.204 nm. Compared with AC-CDs, the diameter of CA-CDs is larger than that of AC-CDs. The size of CA-CDs is centered at 3.98 ± 0.37 nm, which may bring about a higher photodegradation capacity.

The crystal and structure features of samples were characterized by XRD measurement. The XRD carves of AC-CDs and CA-CDs are shown in Fig. 4a. The pattern of CA-CDs is almost consistent with that of AC-CDs. The diffraction peak located at about 30° corresponds to the (200) plane of graphitic carbon [63]. The peak of AC-CDs clearly shifts to the lower-angle direction, which is ascribed to the differences in the crystallinity and crystal distance. For TNs, the typical peaks from 25.28° to 75.03° correspond to the crystal features of anatase TiO2 (PDF No.00–021–1272) [64]. However, CDs/TNs and TNs possess similar distinct peaks. Furthermore, it is worth noticing that there is no characteristic peak of CDs for TNs-based photocatalysts, indicating the introduction of CDs does not produce new crystal structure in the deposition process [65].

FTIR was applied to study the composition groups and surface chemistry of the prepared photocatalysts (Fig. 4b). The peak emerged at 3345 cm−1 in all carves is ascribed to the stretching of O-H bond, suggesting the outstanding hydrophilicity of CDs. The two peaks located at 2700–3000 cm−1 in the spectrum of AC-CDs are indexed to vibration of C-H bond, which are absence for CA-CDs, indicating the difference formation of functional groups between CA-CDs and AC-CDs during preparation. Whereas the peaks observed at 3243 and 1406 in CDs are attributed to the N-H and CO bonds, indicating that there are carbonyl and amide groups on the surface of CDs [65,66]. For CA-CDs, the peak intensity of 3243 cm−1 becomes stronger, verifying the content of N-H is increased [67]. Moreover, the peak at about 1637 cm−1 is considered to be Ti-OH band [68]. Additionally, in the spectrum of CDs/TNs, the characteristic peaks of CDs and TNs are all detected, proving the existence of CDs on the surface of TNs.

XPS technique was employed to justify the chemical groups of the composition elements in 0.2CA-CDs/TNs and 0.2AC-CDs/TNs. Above all, the XPS results of 0.2CA-CDs and 0.2AC-CDs are compared. From Fig. 5a, it can be observed that the 0.2CA-CDs and 0.2AC-CDs contain the elements C, N, and O. However, a detailed examination implies that the atom ratio of C and N in 0.2CA-CDs are higher than that of 0.2AC-CDs, which may attribute to the different precursors. The C1s spectrum of CDs is divided into five peaks located at 284.7 eV, 285.5–285.7 eV, 286.3 eV, 287.5 eV and 288.5 eV, which belong to CC/C-C, C-N, C-O, CN, and CO (Fig. 5b). Compared with 0.2AC-CDs, the content of C-O in 0.2CA-CDs decreases and the CO group shifts to a high energy. Fig. 5c display the N1s spectrum of CDs, where three peaks at ~398.3, ~399.1 and 400.2 eV can be ascribed to C-N-C，N-C3 and N-H. Notably, for 0.2CA-CDs, the peaks of C-N-C，N-C3 and N-H shift, indicating slightly decrease in the electron density. Furthermore, the contents of the three groups in 0.2CA-CDs sharply decrease, which is agreement with the atom ratio of N. From O1s spectra of 0.2AC-CDs and 0.2CA-CDs, 0.2AC-CDs fitted into three peaks with 531.5, 531.8 and 533 eV are corresponding to oxygen adsorption and C-O-C/C-OH (Fig. 5d). For 0.2CA-CDs, the energy of oxygen adsorption is not occurred. Both FTIR and XPS results verify that the chemical groups and electron states are changed among 0.2AC-CDs and 0.2CA-CDs.

Subsequently, the XPS spectra of 0.2CA-CDs/TNs and 0.2AC-CDs/TNs are illustrated in Fig. 6a-d. The full spectra presented in Fig. 6a testify that 0.2CA-CDs/TNs and 0.2AC-CDs/TNs are composed of C, O, and Ti. Particularly, as exhibited in C1s spectrum (Fig. 6b), the separated binding energies of 0.2CA-CDs/TNs and 0.2AC-CDs/TNs are similar. The C1s spectrum fitted into four peaks at 288.2–287.1 eV, 287.3–286.3 eV, 284.9 eV and 283.7–283.5 eV are corresponding to CC, C-O, C-C&CC (graphite), and C-Ti respectively, suggesting the existence of CDs on the surface of CDs/TNs. Compared with 0.2AC-CDs/TNs, negative shifts of CC and C-O are recorded in the 0.2CA-CDs/TNs, manifesting the electron change of C element. In Fig. 6c, the high resolution O1s spectrum is divided into three main peaks at 528.7, 529.4, and 531.4 eV, which can be attributed to Ti-O, C-O, H-O, respectively. Besides, in the Ti2p region (Fig. 6d), the binding energies centered at 457.6 eV and 463 eV can be attributed to Ti2p 3/2 and Ti2p 1/2. Table S1 (Supporting information) lists the atomic ratio of C, N, O, Ti of 0.2AC-CDs/TNs and 0.2CA-CDs/TNs. The atomic ratio of C, O, Ti of 0.2AC-CDs/TNs and 0.2CA-CDs/TNs are 15.05%, 56.09%, 27.38% and 14.01%, 57.02%, 27.64%, respectively. Combined with TEM, FTIR and XPS, the results show that the precursor plays a significant role in forming functional groups and crystal lattice of CDs.

It is well known that the deposition of CDs not only assists TNs enhance the utilization of spectrum light illumination, but also impedes the recombination behavior of electrons and holes. As a result, CDs could facilitate the prompt charge separation and transportation, enhancing the photocatalytic activities of CDs/TNs. To intuitively verify the assumption, the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and transient photocurrent responses (i-t) of TNs, 0.2AC-CDs/TNs, and 0.2CA-CDs/TNs were performed under visible light irradiation. The semicircle at the high frequency part is related to the charge-transfer behavior at the photoelectrode interface, and a narrower arc radius indicates the fast charge transfer rate. From EIS results we can see that pure TNs exhibit the biggest arc radius, manifesting the highest charge transfer resistance across pure TNs ( Fig. 7a). However, the arc radius of 0.2CA-CDs/TNs is overwhelmingly narrower than that of 0.2AC-CDs/TNs, implying the rapidly charges transfer rate when modified by CA-CDs. Another interesting verification comes from the photocurrent response results. The transient photocurrent responses spectra of TNs, 0.2AC-CDs/TNs, and 0.2CA-CDs/TNs are displayed in Fig. 7b. In contrast to pure TNs and 0.2AC-CDs/TNs, 0.2CA-CDs/TNs present the highest photocurrent intensity under visible light irradiation, which is about 18 times as much as that of TNs, revealing that CDs have a positive effect on ROS production. Both EIS and I-t results illustrate that CA-CDs could greatly accelerate the separation and migration of carriers and enhance the photodegradation efficiency of CDs/TNs.

To unveil the photocatalytic efficiency of CDs/TNs with different CDs precursors, a series of degradation tests were performed by employing CR, RhB, and TC as representative pollutants under visible light irradiation. Above all, the corresponding kinetics of photodegradation with different samples are portrayed in the following equation:
(1) -kt=ln (C/C0)
where C, C0, and k are the initial concentration, actual concentration of dyes at reaction time, and rate constants, respectively.

As a vital component in CDs/TNs, appropriate amount CDs have inestimable influence on improving photocatalytic activity of catalysts. Thus, the photocatalytic abilities of CDs/TNs with different CDs contents were investigated. The results are illustrated in Fig. 8(a-f). The degradation efficiencies of CA-CDs/TNs and AC-CDs/TNs composites toward the decomposition of CR, RhB, and TC were compared under visible light irradiation. For AC-CDs/TNs (Fig. 8(a-c)), CR, RhB, and TC are hardly degraded under visible light irradiation with pure TNs. The removal efficiency of AC-CDs/TNs notably enhance with increasing the amount of CDs from 0 to 0.2 M. After that, the degradation efficiency does not improve continuously. This inhibiting result may be attributed to the photo-shielding effect of CDs, which happens due to the competition for photons with TNs. The relationships between -ln (Ct/C0) and t of TNs, AC-CDs/TNs and CA-CDs/TNs are illustrated in Fig. 9(a-c) and
Tables 1–2. For AC-CDs/TNs, 0.2 AC-CDs/TNs show the highest k values of 0.01381 min−1, 0.01295 min−1, and 0.02483 min−1 for CR, RhB, and TC, which are nearly 142.49, 81.34, and 8.09 times than those of TNs. In terms of TNs based photocatalysts, the k value follows the sequence of k 0.2> k 0.1> k 0.3> k 0.4> k TNs. For CA-CDs/TNs, the highest k values (CR 0.01807 min−1, RhB 0.01587 min−1, and TC 0.02883 min−1) are nearly 1.31, 1.23, and 1.16 times than those of 0.2 AC-CDs/TNs. The k value of CR, RhB, and TC is in order of k 0.2> k 0.15> k 0.1> k 0.25> k 0.3> k TNs. From the results we can see that 0.2CDs/TNs present the highest photodegradation rate. Besides, the degradation efficiency of the three pollutants of AC-CDs/TNs and CA-CDs/TNs follows the pseudo-first-order kinetics.

Further, the degradation performance of CA-CDs/TNs are shown in Figs. 8(d-e) and 9(d-e). As expected, with the amount of CDs increasing, the degradation ability and rate constant are obviously improved. Further increasing the amount of CDs will result in a significant decrease of photocatalytic capacity. The degradation process is hold back due to the less valid decomposition of pollutant into small molecules at high CA-CDs amount. Consequently, the optimized dosage of CDs is determined as 0.2 M in the further experiments. Meanwhile, the above results indicate that after decorating CDs onto TNs, both 0.2AC-CDs/TNs and 0.2CA-CDs/TNs can be effective stimulated under visible light.

It is difficult to achieve a higher degradation efficiency when the degradation process is almost completed. Thus, the final degradation abilities of 0.2CA-CDs/TNs and 0.2AC-CDs/TNs were compared. As presented in Fig. 10, 0.2CA-CDs/TNs show a higher photodegradation efficiencies under visible light condition. The photodegraded CR is about 91.6% after 2 h, and the k value (0.01807 min−1) is 1.31 times than that of 0.2AC-CDs/TNs (0.01381 min−1). What’s more, 0.2CA-CDs/TNs also display a better degradation efficiency (90.2%) than 0.2AC-CDs/TNs (83.07%) for removing RhB. Further, approximately 96.1% of TC is still effectively removed at 60 min in the existence of 0.2CA-CDs/TNs. The k value is 0.02883 min−1, which is around 1.16 than the 0.2AC-CDs/TNs (0.02483 min−1). The degradation efficiency of 0.2CA-CDs/TNs achieved 91.6%, 90.2%, and 96.1% toward CR, RhB, and TC which are 5.7%, 7.13%, and 6.7% higher than that of 0.2AC-CDs/TNs. Based on the results, 0.2CA-CDs/TNs possess greater photodegradation ability.

In order to verify the wide application of 0.2CA-CDs/TNs toward the same structure of pollutants, triphenylmethane structure- BG and azo structure-ZB ( Fig. 11a) with the concentration of 20 mg. L-1 and 100 mg. L−1 were used to access the degradation property of 0.2CA-CDs/TNs. As shown in Fig. 11b, about 91.08% of BG and 86.69% of ZB are removed by 0.2CA-CDs/TNs. For BG degradation, the absorption peak centered at 623 nm descends gradually with the increase of degradation time. Moreover, the inset image in Fig. 11b reveals that BG solution is discolored completely by 0.2CA-CDs/TNs. The absorption carves resulting from photodegradation of ZB are shown in Fig. 11c. The absorption intensities at 507 nm sharply decrease, illustrating that ZB is degraded as the reaction time prolongs. Taken into consideration, these results reveal that 0.2CA-CDs/TNs have excellent photocatalytic activity for degrading various pollutants.

Further, according to the previous report, the bandgap energy of the 0.2 CA-CDs/TNs is accessed [69]. The photon energy hv is calculated in the following equation:

where A, Eg, and n are the energy-independent constant, the optical band gap energy, and a constant (directly allowed transition n = 0.5), respectively. Besides, t represents the thickness of samples (0.5 mm), and hv is 0 when extrapolating the linear portion of these plots to photon energy. Ultimately, compared with TNs (3.2 eV), the prepared 0.2CA-CDs/TNs exhibits a narrow bandgap energy of 1.52 eV, indicating 0.2CA-CDs/TNs can be excited under visible light irradiation in theory ( Fig. 12).

The BET specific surface areas of 0.2 CA-CDs/TNs and 0.2 AC-CDs/TNs are about 129.63 and 137.72 m2/g respectively, which can be ascribed to the large surface area of TNs and the different sizes of CDs (Table S2). Besides, compared with 0.2 AC-CDs/TNs, 0.2 CA-CDs/TNs present a higher textural property, which is consistent with the photodegradation results.

Consequently, the unstable fragments and final products of CR, RhB, TC and ZB as well as BG at different photodegradation times were analyzed by LC-MS. CR and BG as representative structures of azo and triphenylmethane are discussed in detail. Plausible molecular structures and relative information regarding CR are listed in Table S3. After degradation for 3 h, a series of intermediate products were detected. Products with m/z 239.01 and 184.24 (4,4′-diaminobiphenyl) result from the cleavage of -NN- moieties. Intermediates with m/z 208.23 (naphthalenesulfonic acid) and 144.17 (naphthol) arise from the oxidation of naphthalene. Products with m/z 158.21 (naphthoquinone) and 108.09 (benzoquinone) are derived from ring opening and mineralization of m/z 144.17 and 110.11. The peak at m/z 169.23 (4-aminobiphenyl) can be attributed to the cleavage of -C-C- in a product with m/z 184.24, and the product with m/z 94.11 (phenol) is the oxidation product of m/z 122.12 (benzoic acid). According to the degradation intermediates, tentative reaction pathways for CR degradation are presented in Fig. 13a.

For BG degradation, products with m/z 355.49 and 272.31 result from the removal of N-ethyl group, forming N-de-ethylated products. Intermediates with m/z 198.21 and 182.21 arise from the cleavage of -C-C- /-CC- moieties. Products with m/z 138.13 (p-hydroxybenzoic acid), 106.12 (benzoic acid) and 110.1 (1，4-phenol) are derived from the mineralization of m/z 198.21 and 182.21. With the degradation process prolonging, the stability of intermediate fragments decreases, resulting in the production of a series of organic acids (Fig. 13e and Table S4).

Apart from CR and BG, the possible degradation pathways of RhB, TC and ZB were also elucidated (Fig. 13b, c and d), starting from the protonated RhB, ZB, and TC molecular ions with m/z 443.23, 502.43 and 444.15, respectively. The fragments generated during the reaction process are shown in Table s6-s7. All the above-mentioned products can be eventually decomposed into CO2, H2O, and other small molecules.

In brief, the photocatalytic removal of azo structure involves (a) cleavage of -NN- and -C-C- bonds, (b) hydroxylation of naphthalene, (c) hydroxylation of the benzene ring, and (d) ring opening and mineralization of the benzene ring. The photocatalytic removal of triphenylmethane structure includes (a) removal of N-ethyl group, (b) the damage of conjugate structure, (c) the hydroxylation of benzene ring, (d) open ring and mineralization. Our work may construct a system guideline to the degradation pathway of azo structure and triphenylmethane structure pollutants.

The photodegradation capacities of 0.2CA-CDs/TNs are compared to the previous reports (Table 1). Considering the different experimental parameters, the prepared 0.2CA-CDs/TNs in this study present outstanding photocatalytic ability among these photocatalysts.

Scheme 2 resumes the overall photodegradation process of CDs/TNs. On the one hand, under visible light illumination, electron-hole pairs are inspired with the photo-excitation of TNs by responding to UV light. Meanwhile, visible light can be converted into UV light by CDs due to its upconverted fluorescent ability, which is subsequently absorbed by TNs. Then, electrons in the CB of TNs readily transmit to the surface of CDs, producing O2 •- by consuming molecular oxygen, which takes part in the degradation process. The holes on the VB of TiO2 oxidize with H2O or OH- to generate •OH, which completely mineralizes the pollutants. On the other hand, CDs may act as electron reservoir and donor in photocatalytic reaction, and it is possible for CDs to capture electrons from the CB band of TNs and then release them to the degradation process. Ultimately, CDs could impede the recombination of e+-h- pairs, improving the photocatalytic capacity of CDs/TNs. ( Table 3).

In this work, different CDs precursors were used to synthesize CDs/TNs by hydrothermal approach. CDs are well uniformly deposited on TNs by Ti-O-C bonds. The introduction of CDs largely stimulates the migration efficiency of photogenerated electrons and holes of TNs. The CDs/TNs show higher photodegradation efficiency and larger rate constant for CR, RhB, and TC than TNs, which can be assigned to the broadened visible light absorption, the prompt separation and transfer of photoinduced charge carriers as well as the hampered recombination possibility of electron-hole pairs originated from the CDs doping effect. Howbeit, among the prepared samples, 0.2CA-CDs/TNs exhibit the highest reaction rate and more than 85% of organic pollutants were decomposed after visible light irradiation. It is believed that our work could offer a beneficial guidance to the utilization of photocatalysts, sparking the application such as wastewater purification as well as energy conversion.
